1: The east gate of the inner court remains shut on working days but opens on Sabbaths and new moons. The rhythmic opening and closing of the gate structures time into ordinary and sacred. A shadow: Christ as the sabbath-rest of God (Hebrews 4:3-10) opens the gate of access to the divine presence on the day of rest that he embodies. The resurrection on the first day of the week transforms the Sabbath-gate theology of Ezekiel 46:1 — the gate of access to YHWH is permanently open in Christ, with the Lord's Day as the enacted celebration of that access.
2: The prince enters through the vestibule, stands at the gate post, and watches the priests offer his offerings — then worships at the threshold. The prince is present at worship but in a defined position, not inside the sanctuary proper. A type: Christ is simultaneously the messianic worshipper who presents offerings (at the Last Supper) and the high priest who performs the offering (on the cross). In the restored order, these two roles converge in one person — prince and priest in the Melchizedekian pattern (Hebrews 7:1-17).
3: The people worship before the LORD at the entrance of that gate on Sabbaths and new moons. The worshipping community gathers at the gate, not inside — still outside the inner court. A shadow: the gathered community at the threshold of the holy presence, worshipping in the outer court of the sanctuary, is the type of the gathered church. In Christ, the community's access is extended: "we have confidence to enter the holy places by the blood of Jesus" (Hebrews 10:19) — the threshold worship of 46:3 becomes the inner-court access of the new covenant.
4: The Sabbath burnt offering: six lambs and a ram without blemish. The Sabbath offering is substantial — six-plus-one, the structure of creation weeks. A shadow: the Sabbath offering of six lambs (representing the six days of work) plus a ram (the crowning rest) is the liturgical structure of creation. Christ fulfills the Sabbath by completing the work of new creation: "It is finished" (John 19:30) echoes "God rested on the seventh day" (Genesis 2:2). The six lambs of labor and the one of completion are the type.
5: The grain offering with the ram is one ephah; with the lambs, as much as he is able — a liberal measure. The flexibility in the lamb-accompanying offering — "as much as he is able" — introduces grace into the regulated system. A revelation of God: even within the legal structure of the offerings, YHWH provides for proportional giving based on capacity. Christ extends this to the principle of grace — the widow's two coins are more than the wealthy man's abundance (Mark 12:43). Giving measured by capacity, not uniformity.
6: On the new moon: a young bull, six lambs, and a ram — the full new moon offering. The new moon marks the beginning of each month — time consecrated at its restart. A shadow: Christ consecrates time at its beginning as the one through whom "all things were created" (Colossians 1:16) and in whom the new creation has begun (2 Corinthians 5:17). The monthly renewal offering of 46:6 is the liturgical shadow of the new creation's permanent consecration in Christ.
7: Grain offering proportions for the new moon: one ephah per bull and per ram, for lambs as much as he gives. The proportional grain offering again. A revelation of God: the regularity and proportion of the offerings reflects the ordered character of YHWH. Christ embodies divine order — "God is not a God of disorder but of peace" (1 Corinthians 14:33). The liturgical regularity of 46:7 is the shadow of the ordered character of the God who is the same yesterday and today and forever.
8: The prince enters by the vestibule of the gate and goes out the same way. Consistent entrance and exit — the prince moves predictably within the sacred geography. A type: Christ's movements are consistent with his identity — he comes from the Father and returns to the Father (John 13:3; Ezekiel 16:28). The disciplined movement of the prince within the sanctuary reflects the theological precision of Christ's descent and ascent.
9: When people come before the LORD at the appointed feasts, whoever enters by the north gate goes out by the south — no turning back through the same gate. Unidirectional movement through the sanctuary. A shadow: the eschatological movement through the presence of God is one-way — transformation is not reversed. Christ's own movement is paradigmatically unidirectional: "he set his face to go to Jerusalem" (Luke 9:51). The movement of the worshipper through the sacred space without retracing steps is the type of discipleship as forward movement.
10: "The prince shall be among them; when they go in, he shall go in, and when they go out, he shall go out." The prince moves with the people — he does not remain above or behind but participates in the community's movement. A direct type: "Immanuel" — God with us — is the Christological fulfillment of Ezekiel 46:10. Christ is among his people when they gather (Matthew 18:20), and goes with them when they scatter on mission (Matthew 28:20). The prince who moves with the people at their pace is the type of the God who accommodates himself to human movement.
11: At feasts and appointed festivals, the grain offering is one ephah per bull and one per ram, with lambs as much as one is able. The repeated "as much as one is able" provision — grace within the law. A revelation of God: YHWH calibrates his demands to human capacity. Christ does not crush the bruised reed (Matthew 12:20); his yoke is easy and his burden light (Matthew 11:30). The capacity-sensitive provisions of 46:11 reflect the divine pastoral concern that Christ embodies.
12: When the prince provides a freewill offering — burnt offering or peace offerings — the gate is opened for him on any day, not just Sabbath and new moon. The freewill offering transcends the calendar. A type: Christ's self-offering is the supreme freewill offering — "No one takes my life from me; I lay it down of my own accord" (John 10:18). The voluntary nature of Christ's death is the substance of which the prince's freewill offering is the shadow. And because of it, the gate is permanently open — access is not restricted to scheduled times but available whenever the freewill gift has been given.
13: "You shall provide a yearling lamb without blemish as a daily burnt offering (olah tamid) to the LORD; you shall provide it every morning." The tamid — the daily continuous burnt offering — is the heartbeat of the Levitical system: one lamb every morning, one every evening (Exodus 29:38-42). A direct type: Christ is the fulfillment of the tamid. "By a single offering he has perfected for all time those who are being sanctified" (Hebrews 10:14). The daily repetition of the tamid is the shadow; Christ's single offering, permanently efficacious, is the substance. The lamb offered every morning in the restored temple is the type of "the Lamb who was slain" who stands before the throne forever (Revelation 5:6).
14: The grain offering with the daily lamb: one-sixth of an ephah with one-third of a hin of oil — a regular perpetual offering every morning. The minhah accompanying the tamid is the regular grain gift. A shadow: the daily bread of the morning offering points toward Christ who is the "bread of life" (John 6:35) — the daily sustenance that YHWH provides for his people. The Lord's Prayer petition "give us this day our daily bread" is the new covenant appropriation of the morning grain-offering logic.
15: "Thus the lamb, the grain offering, and the oil shall be provided morning by morning as a regular burnt offering." The tamid system stated as a permanent ordinance. The daily, morning, unceasing character of the offering is its essential quality. A shadow: Christ is the one whose intercession "ever lives" (Hebrews 7:25) — not offered once and done, but permanently effective, permanently presented. The unceasing morning sacrifice is the shadow of the intercessory life of the risen Christ before the Father.
16: "If the prince makes a gift to any of his sons as an inheritance, it shall belong to his sons; it is their property by inheritance." The prince's inheritance gift to sons is permanent. A shadow: the Father's gift to the Son — and through the Son to those who are "sons" by adoption — is permanent. "Your Father has been pleased to give you the kingdom" (Luke 12:32). The sonship inheritance of 46:16 is the type of the adoption that makes believers co-heirs with Christ (Romans 8:17).
17: "But if he makes a gift from his inheritance to one of his servants, it shall belong to him only until the year of liberty; then it shall revert to the prince." The servant's gift is temporary — reverted at the year of jubilee. A shadow: the contrast between permanent sonship-inheritance and temporary servant-gift is the spatial image of Paul's Galatians 4 argument: "you are no longer a slave but a son, and if a son, then an heir through God" (Galatians 4:7). The jubilee reversion of the servant's gift illustrates the temporal limit of what is received apart from sonship. In Christ, the adoption that makes slaves sons grants permanent, non-reversible inheritance.
18: "The prince shall not take any of the people's inheritance, dispossessing them of their property. He shall give his sons their inheritance from his own property, so that none of my people are dispersed from their property." The prince who does not dispossess. A type: Christ, the ultimate Prince, does not take what belongs to others but gives from what is his own. His atonement is the giving of his own life so that the dispossessed regain their inheritance. "He who was rich became poor for our sake, so that through his poverty you might become rich" (2 Corinthians 8:9).
19: The guide brings Ezekiel to the entrance beside the gate, to the north row of holy chambers for the priests — the place where the priests eat the most holy offerings. The tour of the priests' kitchen area. A revelation of God: YHWH attends to the practical provisions for priestly sustenance. The holiness of the priests' eating — consuming the sin offerings and guilt offerings in the inner court — is as sacred as the offering itself. Christ's instituting of the Lord's Supper in an upper room with practical bread and wine reflects the same divine concern for the embodied sustenance of those who serve.
20: "This is where the priests shall boil the guilt offering and the sin offering and bake the grain offering, so as not to bring them out to the outer court and make the holy places holy." The atonement-offerings are prepared in the inner sanctum, not carried outside. The holiness is contained — not because it is weak but to prevent casual exposure of the people to what requires priestly mediation. A shadow: Christ's atoning work is accomplished in the "inner sanctuary" of the divine council before being applied to those outside. The once-for-all sacrifice was made "outside the city gate" (Hebrews 13:12) — but the preparation, the eternal plan, was in the eternal holy counsel.
21: Outer court cooking areas in each of the four corners — enclosed courts. The practical logistics of temple service spread across the full compass. A revelation of God: YHWH's concern for ordered service extends to practical kitchen arrangements. The God who attends to the architectural details of the eschatological temple is the same God who attends to sparrows (Matthew 10:29) and the number of hairs on a head (Matthew 10:30). Thoroughness in detail is a divine attribute expressed in Christ's pastoral care for his flock.
22: Enclosed courts, forty by thirty cubits, same dimensions at all four corners. Uniformity and regularity in the service areas. A revelation of God: the ordered character of YHWH extends to the spaces where worship is supported. Uniformity prevents favoritism; regular dimensions prevent privilege. Christ establishes the same level-ground principle: "there is no Jew or Greek, slave or free, male or female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus" (Galatians 3:28). The equal courts of 46:22 are the spatial image of equal access in Christ.
23: A row of masonry around each court with boiling places built at the base. The built infrastructure of regular worship. A revelation of God: sustainable worship requires infrastructure — not improvised arrangements but designed, permanent provision for the community's ongoing needs. Christ provides for the ongoing needs of his church through the gifts of the Spirit (1 Corinthians 12:4-11) and the ministry of word and sacrament — permanent structures for the sustenance of the new temple community.
24: "These are the kitchens where those who serve at the temple shall boil the sacrifices of the people." The final verse of the tour identifies the function of the cooking areas: where those who serve boil the people's sacrifices. A revelation of God: service to YHWH includes practical, unglorious work — kitchen duty for the sake of the worshipping community. Christ washes feet (John 13:5) and serves the disciples at table. The kitchen workers of 46:24 are the type of the servant leadership that Christ models and demands from his followers.
